story:
What is love? Anno Domini 934. Under King Aethelstan’s invasion, Aedan of the Wumiss clan led his exhausted family—ten souls from elders to babies—into the northern woods of Scotland, hunters on their heels. He knew the men chasing them would not stop; envy and coveting had lit the fire, and now it would burn until fed with blood. Aedan looked to his father, Angus, and his uncle, Ian. With a nod, the three agreed to a hard choice.

“Father, uncle, we will mislead them. Perhaps fortune will favor us.” He embraced his kin, kissed his wife, Sandy, and his children. “Sandy, this is it. Endure. Survive. Thrive, so the Wumiss may live.”

Sandy clung to him, her silent tears a cry the world could hear. Then, while she led the clan upstream toward the tip of Scotland—what is now called John o’ Groats—the three men ran downstream toward the region of Wick, staying on dry ground to lay a clear trail for the hounds. By dusk, Sandy and the Wumiss had launched a small boat and crossed to the island now known as Hoy. In her heart she knew Aedan, Angus, and Ian had offered the last full measure. On that shore she swore: “We will survive, endure, and thrive, and one day return free, strong, and proud Scottish.”

Anno Domini 1296. King Edward I marches into Scotland. On Hoy, Alistair Wumiss, heir to Sandy’s grit, prospered. His family’s single boat had become a fleet of ten, most travel to and from Hoy now carried by his cogs. One night, merry with heather honey mead, his eye caught a hooded figure at the far corner of the inn. She left, and Alistair followed to the fountain, where a steward thanked him for a meeting. The lady lifted her hood. Adeline—bright as a new dawn—asked passage for herself and her father from Hoy to Calais, at once, for a private hire. A small purse of twenty gold pounds spoke to urgency more than price. Alistair bowed. “It shall be done.”

Aboard the Caledonian Dream, Alistair and Adeline shared shy glances and morning talk that lengthened into something neither named and both felt. Then the cry: English warboats from the east. Alistair took the helm, driving for the coast, threading treacherous rocks until one enemy ship foundered. But the net drew tight. Arrows fell. Adeline’s father and a crewman died.

Adeline stepped forward, placing herself between steel and the Wumiss crew. “Alistair, my love,” she said with the clear sorrow of one who chooses duty, “I blinded myself to happy days. We cannot be together. King Edward wants me living to bargain with my uncle, the king of France.” She would not let more men die for her. “Live for me. Be stronger. Keep your ancestors' vow. Thrive.”

Alistair would have fought the sea itself, but he saw the truth in her plea. She surrendered, and her choice spared the crew.

Years later, some called Alistair Wumiss blessed, some claimed dark bargains, others whispered of high patrons. He only walked Hoy’s familiar street, entered the old inn, and asked for the finest honey mead. “What became of the young lady?” the innkeeper wondered. Alistair smiled at a memory that hurt and healed. “She was carried to her destiny, and it was the best time of my life.” What is love, if not to love without fear—even when the world will not let you keep the one your heart has chosen?
